channel.title: NYT > Climate and Environment
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Nature Report, Killed by Trump, Is Released Independently
item.description: A draft assessment of the health of nature in the United States is grim but shot through with bright spots and possibility.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn
item.category: National Academies of Sciences, Engineering and Medicine
item.media:credit: David Robert Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: A dried part of the Arkansas River in Garden City, Kan.

item.title: Should New York City Burn Its Parks? This Scientist Thinks So.
item.description: An unusual outbreak of wildfires in city parks gave scientists a chance to study these rare events. Now they’re coming to different conclusions.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey and Karsten Moran
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times

item.title: Why Data Center Firms Are Working With Trump on Energy Costs
item.description: The White House has floated a new plan to try to ease voter concerns over the A.I. boom’s effect on electric bills. But it won’t be easy to put into practice.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Christie Hemm Klok for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Google data center. Large data centers can use as much electricity as a small city.

item.title: Sea Levels Are Already Higher Than Many Scientists Think, New Study Shows
item.description: Researchers found that a majority of studies on coastal sea levels underestimated how high water levels are, and hundreds of millions of people are closer to peril than previously thought.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey and Mira Rojanasakul
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: A stretch of coast in Suriname, one of many places affected by sea level rise and erosion caused by climate change

item.title: From 2011: Wangari Maathai, Nobel Peace Prize Laureate, Dies at 71
item.description: A Kenyan environmentalist, she began by paying women a few shillings to plant trees and went on to become the first African woman to win a Nobel Peace Prize.
item.dc:creator: Jeffrey Gettleman
item.media:description: Wangari Maathai in 2005, with a tree she planted in the Newlands Forest in Cape Town, South Africa.

item.title: Potomac River in D.C. Is Safe, Officials Say. But Locals Still Worry About the Poop.
item.description: A broken pipe sent a gusher of sewage into the river near Washington. Some people may try to row, sail or fish. But skeptics are steering clear.
item.dc:creator: Amy Qin and Salwan Georges
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: The collapse of a major sewer line sent 243 million gallons of raw human waste flowing into the Potomac. Here, a pipe reroutes wastewater.

item.title: Feeling the Effects of 260,000 Federal Jobs Lost
item.description: One year in, assessing budget cuts to federal climate and science jobs.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.media:description: Demonstrators gathered to protest against budget cuts outside the headquarters of the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration in Silver Spring, Maryland, last year.

item.title: On the Trendy Tram, You Can Hear a Subway Voice
item.description: A man who has done voice-overs for the M.T.A. for years is now speaking to riders of the Roosevelt Island Tram.
item.dc:creator: James Barron

item.title: Trump’s High-Profile Oil and Gas Lease Sale in Alaska Has No Takers
item.description: No companies bid for the chance to drill in more than 1 million acres of water off Alaska’s Cook Inlet, the Interior Department said Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.category: Bureau of Ocean Energy Management, Regulation and Enforcement
item.media:credit: Nathaniel Wilder for The New York Times
item.media:description: An oil platform in the Cook Inlet last year.

item.title: As New York Energy Costs Surge, Attention Turns to Landmark Climate Law
item.description: The battle to lower costs has reached the State Capitol, where concerns have emerged about the fate of a 2019 climate law and its ambitious goals to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
item.dc:creator: Hilary Howard
item.media:credit: Tom Sibley for The New York Times
item.media:description: Solar panels line a rooftop in Astoria, Queens. New York ranks among the most expensive states for electricity in the United States.

item.title: Liquid Natural Gas Exporters Like Shell and ExxonMobil Could See Big Profits From War
item.description: Prices for liquefied natural gas have shot up in recent days, which could bring bigger profits for European and U.S. energy companies.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: A liquefied natural gas production facility in Qatar this week. Companies like Shell, TotalEnergies and ExxonMobil could benefit from the war.

item.title: TerraPower Nuclear Reactor in Wyoming Gets Federal Permit
item.description: TerraPower’s project in Wyoming, which uses novel technology, is the first new commercial reactor to receive federal approval in nearly a decade.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Benjamin Rasmussen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Politicians and executives, including Bill Gates, shoveled dirt during the groundbreaking for TerraPower’s Natrium nuclear power plant in Kemmerer, Wyo.

item.title: Kristi Noem Review Delayed FEMA Aid by Weeks, Senate Finds
item.description: A report by Senate Democrats found that scrutiny of large FEMA expenditures slowed housing and employment assistance for victims of fires and storms.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:description: Kristi Noem, the homeland security secretary, at the Federal Emergency Management Agency headquarters in Washington in January.

item.title: Who Could Profit From an Energy Crisis?
item.description: A protracted conflict in the Middle East could seriously disrupt energy markets.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.media:description: A Cheniere Energy liquefied natural gas plant in Port Arthur, Texas.

item.title: Potomac River Reopens for Some Uses After Sewer Collapse
item.description: Contamination levels in the waterway have declined, but health advisories remain in place for boaters downstream.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:description: A volunteer looks at a section of the Potomac River before collecting a sample of water to test for E.coli on Feb. 19, 2026, in Cabin John, Md.

item.title: Monterey Bay Aquarium Seafood Watch Provide Sustainable Recommendations
item.description: Seafood Watch, long a source of advice on sustainable choices, is working to get restaurants to adopt its color-coded recommendations.
item.dc:creator: Kim Severson
item.media:credit: Erin Brethauer for The New York Times
item.media:description: William Dissen, the owner of the Market Place Restaurant in Asheville, N.C., serves farmed trout from a local fishery.

item.title: Oil and Natural Gas Prices Keep Climbing as Iran Attacks Escalate
item.description: Energy prices continued to rise on Tuesday as attacks by the United States and Israel on Iran intensified, along with the country’s response.
item.dc:creator: Kailyn Rhone
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Drivers queuing at a gas station in Los Angeles on Monday. The price of gas was up by about 11 cents a gallon on Tuesday morning.

item.title: Trump Bets That ‘Energy Dominance’ Will Make War in Iran Less Costly
item.description: So far, the energy price shocks have been relatively contained. But a prolonged conflict in the Middle East carries enormous economic risks.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer, Lisa Friedman and Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: J. Emilio Flores for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kern River Oil fields near Bakersfield, Calif.

item.title: Scientists Decry ‘Political Attack’ on the Reference Manual on Scientific Evidence
item.description: More than two dozen contributors to the manual criticized the deletion of a chapter on climate science by the Federal Judicial Center.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.category: National Academies of Sciences, Engineering and Medicine
item.media:description: The Thurgood Marshall Federal Judiciary Building where the Federal Judicial Center is located.

item.title: A Crisis in the Alps: Airbnb, Climate Change and Americans
item.description: The mountains’ resort towns have reached an inflection point, facing changes that threaten their cultures and even survival, as demand for short-term rentals reshapes the landscape.
item.dc:creator: Sophie Stuber

item.title: Oil Prices Surge After Iran Attack
item.description: How long prices remain high will depend on what the United States, Israel and Iran do next.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
item.media:description: An oil tanker anchored near Oman was ablaze on Sunday in this photo taken from a video.

item.title: Can Nations Agree How to Mine the Sea? This Is the Year, She Says.
item.description: Leticia Carvalho heads a global authority that’s been struggling to set rules for a decade. President Trump’s aggressive push on ocean mining makes her task more urgent.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:credit: Natalie Keyssar for The New York Times

item.title: Caesar Rodney statue to get place of honor in Washington
item.description: Caesar Rodney, whose statue was placed in storage amid racial injustice protests in 2020, will be honored in Washington this summer.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:description: A bronze statue of Caesar Rodney, a Declaration of Independence signer, on a galloping horse stood in downtown Wilmington, Del., for nearly 100 years until it was removed in 2020.

item.title: Judge Approves $345 Million Verdict Against Greenpeace in Dakota Access Pipeline Suit
item.description: Greenpeace has said the verdict could bankrupt it. The lawsuit was over the group’s role in protests against the Dakota Access Pipeline.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:description: Protesters against the Dakota Access Pipeline in North Dakota in 2016.

item.title: Fighting Wildfires Could Soon Get Harder
item.description: Simultaneous emergencies in different parts of the world could stop countries from sharing ground crews and equipment, new research warns.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Dzombak
item.media:description: A wildfire this month in Chubut Province, Argentina. Different parts of the world have historically had different fire seasons, allowing regions to rotate resources.

item.title: With a New Nigeria Refinery, Africa’s Richest Man Wants ‘to Rescue the Country’
item.description: Aliko Dangote, Africa’s richest man, has an ambitious vision for the continent’s most populous nation, but hurdles stand in his way.
item.dc:creator: Dionne Searcey
item.media:credit: Victor Adewale for The New York Times
item.media:description: Aliko Dangote at home in Lagos, Nigeria, last month.

item.title: Vanguard Settles Case Claiming It Tried to Kill the Coal Industry
item.description: Republican-led states had accused financial firms of colluding against coal producers. Vanguard also agreed not to push for action to fight climate change.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:description: Ken Paxton, the Texas attorney general who filed the suit in 2024.

item.title: Epstein Is Shaking the Climate World, Too
item.description: A major funder of the Earthshot Prize has been linked to the convicted sex offender.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:description: Prince William, left, with Sultan Ahmed bin Sulayem in Dubai in 2022.

item.title: Bird Losses Are Accelerating, New Study Finds
item.description: Scientists studying data collected over more than three decades found accelerating losses. Their research offers clues about the causes.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn
item.media:description: The steepest losses in the study, from 1987 to 2021, were seen in Florida, Texas, Louisiana and Arizona.

item.title: Space X Wants to Launch 1 Million Satellites, but Is There Room?
item.description: Elon Musk wants to launch a million satellites, but researchers say global warming is changing the upper atmosphere in ways that makes space junk linger.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:description: An Ariane 5 rocket launched from French Guiana last year. Its two satellites joined the roughly 13,000 already in orbit.

item.title: Antarctica’s Mineral Riches Exposed as Climate Warms
item.description: Mining is banned on the frozen continent. But new research suggests that could change as ice melts and land and valuable minerals are exposed.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.media:description: The Transantarctic Mountains, which, along with sites on the Antarctic Peninsula, hold the most promising mineral deposits in Antarctica.

item.title: Bird Flu Strikes California Elephant Seals for the First Time
item.description: Thirty seals, primarily weaned pups, have died since late last week, scientists said.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes and Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: Elephant seals at Año Nuevo State Park in Pescadero, Calif., last month.

item.title: Trump Says Tech Firms Should Pay More for Electricity
item.description: The president said he had negotiated a deal with tech giants to cover the energy costs of data centers, but offered few details. Experts said such pledges could prove difficult in practice.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Rebecca Noble for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Microsoft data center under construction in Goodyear, Ariz., last month.

item.title: Trump’s Pick to Manage Public Lands Pledges No Mass Sell-Off
item.description: Steve Pearce, President Trump’s nominee to lead the Bureau of Land Management, has a long history of pushing to privatize federal property.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:description: Steve Pearce, the nominee for the Bureau of Land Management, during a news conference in 2023 in Albuquerque, N.M., when he was the chair of the state’s Republican Party.

item.title: Plastic, Plastic Everywhere
item.description: We talk to the author of a new book about why the problem is so hard to solve.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.category: Plastic Inc.: The Secret History and Shocking Future of Big Oil's Biggest Bet (Book)
item.media:description: “If you go to the supermarket and you buy a bunch of bananas and they come wrapped in plastic, you did not demand plastic wrapping,” the author Beth Gardiner said.

item.title: Judge Axes Exxon’s Defamation Suit Against Environmentalists
item.description: But the case against the California attorney general, prompted by his lawsuit over Exxon’s plastic recycling program, can proceed in Texas federal court.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:description: The Exxon Mobil Baytown refinery in Texas last month.

item.title: Billions in Climate Grants, Frozen for a Year, Are Back in Court
item.description: A year after the Trump administration blocked money that had been awarded by Congress for clean energy projects, the legal fight over the funds rages on.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.category: U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia
item.media:description: Last year, Lee Zeldin, E.P.A. administrator, sought to cancel $20 billion in clean energy grants that were awarded to eight nonprofits under the Biden administration.

item.title: Norway’s Century-Long Watch on the Northern Lights
item.description: The nation’s northern region has led the scientific quest to understand the aurora borealis. This summer, a 10,000-antenna radar is expected to begin the next phase of exploration.
item.dc:creator: Alexa Robles-Gil and Michal Siarek
item.media:credit: Michał Siarek for The New York Times

item.title: What the Snowstorm Looked Like Across the Northeast
item.description: Record-breaking amounts of snow fell in many parts of the region, blanketing the area with snow in the second large storm of the year.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times and Judson Jones
item.media:credit: José A. Alvarado Jr. for The New York Times

item.title: More Than Half a Million Customers Have Lost Power on East Coast
item.description: The outages were mostly caused by problems with local power lines, and coastal Massachusetts was particularly hard hit.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn and Jenna Russell
item.media:credit: Lucy Lu for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nearly 300,000 electric utility customers in Massachusetts were without power at noon on Monday.

item.title: The Evolution of Eyes Began With One
item.description: Even Charles Darwin was puzzled by the evolution of the vertebrate eye. New research suggests that it traces back to a cyclopean invertebrate with a single eye atop the head.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer
item.media:description: Scientists believe invertebrates 560 million years ago had a single eye at the top of their head, which only later split in two.

item.title: Supreme Court to Weigh Oil-Industry Effort to End a Major Climate Suit
item.description: The case could have significant bearing on a range of other lawsuits brought against the fossil fuel industry by cities and states across the country.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Rachel Ellis/The Denver Post, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A Suncor Energy oil refinery in Commerce City, Colo.

item.title: Cities May Be ‘Evolutionary Training Grounds’ For Spotted Lanternflies
item.description: Living in urban China may have given the insects the traits they needed to thrive in the United States, a new study suggests.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Spotted lanternflies, native to Shanghai, have spread rapidly throughout the Eastern United States since they were first discovered there in 2014, invading about 20 states and counting.

item.title: Thousands of Flights Are Canceled as Storm Slams Northeast Airports
item.description: Airlines suspended operations at some major airports and expect to restart some flights on Monday and more on Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi and Ceylan Yeğinsu
item.category: Port Authority of New York and New Jersey
item.media:credit: Dakota Santiago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Snow-covered parts of a runway at Newark Liberty International Airport on Sunday night.

item.title: Is Climate Change Making Inflation Worse?
item.description: There’s mounting evidence that extreme weather is making some everyday stuff more expensive. But how that plays out for you depends on several factors.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by The New York Times; Shutterstock

item.title: A Post-Katrina Law Guards FEMA Resources. Why Hasn’t It Stopped Noem?
item.description: A key statute was designed to rein in the Homeland Security secretary and prevent deadly mistakes. Lawyers say its provisions are difficult to enforce.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:description: The homeland security secretary, Kristi Noem, during a visit to the FEMA headquarters in Washington on Jan. 24.
